name: Banu Musa Brothers
knownFor: Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi, or simply al-Khwarizmi was a mathematician active during the Islamic Golden
intro: The Banū Mūsā brothers, Ahmad, Muhammad, and al-Hasan, were a group of three Persian scholars who lived during the 9th century in Baghdad, which was then part of the Abbasid Caliphate. They were renowned for their contributions to various fields, including mathematics, mechanics, and engineering. The brothers authored several influential works, such as the "Book of Ingenious Devices," which covered a wide range of subjects like automata, geometry, and astronomical instruments. Their works were instrumental in preserving and advancing scientific knowledge from the Hellenistic and Persian traditions.
longBio: Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi, or simply al-Khwarizmi was a mathematician active during the Islamic Golden Age, who produced Arabic-language works in mathematics, astronomy, and geography. Around 820, he worked at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad, the contemporary capital city of the Abbasid Caliphate. One of the most prominent scholars of the period, his works were widely influential on later authors, both in the Islamic world and Europe.
achievement: Their book "The Book of Ingenious Devices" showcased mechanical inventions, automata, and scientific knowledge. Their contributions to mathematics, engineering, and technology had a lasting impact on the Islamic Golden Age and advanced scientific understanding.
funFacts: ["His popularizing treatise on algebra, compiled between 813 and 833 as Al-Jabr (The Compendious Book on Calculation by Completion and Balancing), presented the first systematic solution of linear and quadratic equations."]

name: Charles Babbage
knownFor: Charles Babbage was an English polymath
intro: Charles Babbage was an English mathematician, philosopher, inventor, and mechanical engineer who is best known for conceiving of the idea of a programmable computer. He designed the "Difference Engine," an automatic mechanical calculator designed to tabulate polynomial functions, and the more complex "Analytical Engine," which was intended to perform any kind of calculation and can be considered an early model for the modern computer.
longBio: Charles Babbage was an English polymath. A mathematician, philosopher, inventor and mechanical engineer, Babbage originated the concept of a digital programmable computer.
achievement: He is best known for his design of the Analytical Engine, a mechanical general-purpose computer that was conceived as a successor to his earlier invention, the Difference Engine. Babbage's Analytical Engine incorporated key principles of modern computing, including the use of punched cards for input and output, a memory unit, and the ability to perform arithmetic and logical operations.

name: Ramón Verea
knownFor: Ramón Silvestre Verea Aguiar y García was a Galician journalist, engineer and writer, known as
intro: Ramón Verea was a Galician journalist, engineer, and writer, known for inventing a calculator with an internal multiplication table in 1878.
longBio: Ramón Silvestre Verea Aguiar y García was a Galician journalist, engineer and writer, known as the inventor of a Calculadora Alicia with an internal multiplication table (1878).
achievement: Invented the world’s first single-motion calculator that could solve complex multiplications as a whole. At the time all previous calculators were only able to multiply several numbers by doing so one at a time.

name: Luitzen Egbertus Jan Brouwer
knownFor: Luitzen Egbertus Jan "Bertus" Brouwer was a Dutch mathematician and philosopher who worked in topology,
intro: Luitzen Egbertus Jan Brouwer, generally known as L.E.J. Brouwer, was a Dutch mathematician and philosopher who worked in the early 20th century. He is best known as the founder of the mathematical philosophy of intuitionism, which asserts that the truth of a mathematical statement is a subjective claim that requires construction or proof by the individual asserting it.
longBio: Luitzen Egbertus Jan "Bertus" Brouwer was a Dutch mathematician and philosopher who worked in topology, set theory, measure theory and complex analysis. Regarded as one of the greatest mathematicians of the 20th century, he is known as one of the founders of modern topology, particularly for establishing his fixed-point theorem and the topological invariance of dimension.
achievement: Known for his contributions to intuitionistic mathematics and the foundations of mathematics. Brouwer's work in constructive mathematics and the philosophy of mathematics had a significant impact on the field, challenging classical notions of mathematical truth and introducing the concept of intuitionistic logic. His foundational ideas continue to influence various branches of mathematics and computer science.

name: Leonardo Torres Quevedo
knownFor: Leonardo Torres Quevedo was a Spanish civil engineer, mathematician and inventor, known for his numerous
intro: Leonardo Torres Quevedo was a Spanish civil engineer, computer scientist, and inventor. He developed the first chess-playing automaton and contributed to the field of modern automation with his inventions of new calculating machines that used electromechanical systems. His machines are considered to be the first digital calculators in history, using discrete variables to do calculations.
longBio: Leonardo Torres Quevedo was a Spanish civil engineer, mathematician and inventor, known for his numerous engineering innovations, including aerial trams, airships, catamarans, and remote control. He was also a pioneer in the field of computing and robotics. Torres was a member of several scientific and cultural institutions and held such important positions as the seat N of the Real Academia Española (1920–1936) and the presidency of the Spanish Royal Academy of Sciences (1928–1934). In 1927 he became a foreign associate of the French Academy of Sciences.
achievement: In 1912, Leonardo Torres Quevedo built El Ajedrecista (the chess player), one of the first autonomous machines capable of playing chess. As opposed to the human-operated The Turk and Ajeeb, El Ajedrecista was a true automaton built to play chess without human guidance. It played an endgame with three chess pieces, automatically moving a white king and a rook to checkmate the black king moved by a human opponent. In his work Essays on Automatics, published in 1914, Torres Quevedo formulates what will be a new branch of engineering: automation and designed an electromechanical version of Babbage's Analytical machine which included floating-point arithmetic.

name: Frank Wilfred Jordan
knownFor: Frank Wilfred Jordan was a British physicist who together with William Henry Eccles invented the
intro: Frank Wilfred Jordan (1881-1941) was a British physicist known for co-inventing the "flip-flop" circuit in 1918 alongside William Henry Eccles. This circuit became fundamental in electronic memory for computers. Jordan received his education at the Royal College of Science and worked as a lecturer in physics. The flip-flop circuit played a crucial role in storing binary data and became a cornerstone of computer technology.
longBio: Frank Wilfred Jordan was a British physicist who together with William Henry Eccles invented the flip-flop circuit in 1918. This circuit became the basis for electronic binary data storage in computers.
achievement: Patented the Eccles–Jordan trigger circuit,the so-called "bistable flip-flop", a building block of all digital memory cells. Built from vacuum tubes, their concept was essential for the success of the Colossus codebreaking computer.

name: Alonzo Church
knownFor: Alonzo Church was an American computer scientist, mathematician, logician, and philosopher who made major contributions
intro: Alonzo Church (1903-1995) was an influential American mathematician and logician. His work in mathematical logic, particularly lambda calculus, laid the foundations for computation theory and functional programming. Church's contributions had a profound impact on computer science and the understanding of computability, influencing fields such as philosophy and artificial intelligence.
longBio: Alonzo Church was an American computer scientist, mathematician, logician, and philosopher who made major contributions to mathematical logic and the foundations of theoretical computer science. He is best known for the lambda calculus, the Church–Turing thesis, proving the unsolvability of the Entscheidungsproblem, the Frege–Church ontology, and the Church–Rosser theorem. Alongside his doctoral student Alan Turing, Church is considered one of the founders of computer science.
achievement: Made fundamental contributions to theoretical computer science, specifically in the development of computability theory in the form of lambda calculus. Independently of Alan Turing, formulated what is now known as the Church-Turing Thesis and proved that first-order logic is undecidable.

name: Alan Turing
knownFor: Alan Mathison Turing was an English mathematician, computer scientist, logician, cryptanalyst, philosopher and theoretical biologist
intro: Alan Turing (1912-1954) was a British mathematician, logician, and computer scientist. He is known for his groundbreaking work in theoretical computer science and artificial intelligence. Turing's contributions to the field include the concept of a universal Turing machine and his role in breaking the Enigma code during World War II.
longBio: Alan Mathison Turing was an English mathematician, computer scientist, logician, cryptanalyst, philosopher and theoretical biologist. He was highly influential in the development of theoretical computer science, providing a formalisation of the concepts of algorithm and computation with the Turing machine, which can be considered a model of a general-purpose computer. Turing is widely considered to be the father of theoretical computer science.
achievement: Made several fundamental contributions to theoretical computer science, including the Turing machine computational model, the conceiving of the stored program concept and the designing of the high-speed ACE design. Independently of Alonzo Church, he formulated the Church-Turing thesis and proved that first-order logic is undecidable. He also explored the philosophical issues concerning artificial intelligence, proposing what is now known as Turing test.

name: George R. Stibitz
knownFor: George Robert Stibitz was an American researcher at Bell Labs who is internationally recognized as
intro: George R. Stibitz (1904-1995) was an American mathematician and electrical engineer. He played a significant role in the early development of digital computing, creating the Complex Number Calculator and contributing to the Bell Labs Model I computer. Stibitz's work was instrumental in shaping the future of computing technology.
longBio: George Robert Stibitz was an American researcher at Bell Labs who is internationally recognized as one of the fathers of the modern digital computer. He was known for his work in the 1930s and 1940s on the realization of Boolean logic digital circuits using electromechanical relays as the switching element.
achievement: Father of modern digital computing and remote job entry. Coined the term "digital". Discovered the reflected binary code known as Gray code. Excess-3 code is named after him as well (Stibitz code).

name: Claude Shannon
knownFor: Claude Elwood Shannon was an American polymath who was a mathematician, electrical engineer, computer scientist,
intro: Claude Shannon (1916-2001) was an influential American mathematician and electrical engineer known as the "father of information theory." His work in digital circuit design and information theory revolutionized the field and laid the foundations for modern telecommunications, cryptography, and computer science. Shannon's contributions continue to shape technology today.
longBio: Claude Elwood Shannon was an American polymath who was a mathematician, electrical engineer, computer scientist, cryptographer, and inventor known as the "father of information theory", and the man who laid the foundations of the Information Age.
achievement: Widely recognized as the "father of information theory." Shannon's groundbreaking work, published in his influential paper "A Mathematical Theory of Communication," established the foundation for modern information theory and laid the groundwork for digital communication and data compression. His concepts of entropy, channel capacity, and coding theory revolutionized the understanding and transmission of information. Shannon's work had a profound impact on various fields, including telecommunications, computer science, cryptography, and beyond.

name: John Mauchly
knownFor: John William Mauchly was an American physicist who, along with J
intro: John Mauchly (1907-1980) was an American physicist and computer scientist. He co-designed and co-built the ENIAC, the world's first general-purpose electronic digital computer, along with J. Presper Eckert. Mauchly's contributions to computer technology and his work on the ENIAC laid the foundation for the development of modern computing.
longBio: John William Mauchly was an American physicist who, along with J. Presper Eckert, designed ENIAC, the first general-purpose electronic digital computer, as well as EDVAC, BINAC and UNIVAC I, the first commercial computer made in the United States.
achievement: With J. Presper Eckert, designed and built the ENIAC, the first modern (all electronic, Turing-complete) computer, and the UNIVAC I, the first commercially available computer. Also worked on BINAC (1949), EDVAC (1949), UNIVAC (1951) with Grace Hopper and Jean Bartik, to develop early stored program computers.

name: John von Neumann
knownFor: John von Neumann was a Hungarian and American mathematician, physicist, computer scientist and engineer
intro: John von Neumann (1903-1957) was a Hungarian-American mathematician, physicist, and computer scientist. He made pioneering contributions to computer architecture and the concept of stored-program computers. Von Neumann's work in mathematics, physics, and game theory solidified his reputation as one of the greatest intellectuals of the 20th century.
longBio: John von Neumann was a Hungarian and American mathematician, physicist, computer scientist and engineer. Von Neumann had perhaps the widest coverage of any mathematician of his time, integrating pure and applied sciences and making major contributions to many fields, including mathematics, physics, economics, computing, and statistics. He was a pioneer in building the mathematical framework of quantum physics, in the development of functional analysis, and in game theory, introducing or codifying concepts including cellular automata, the universal constructor and the digital computer. His analysis of the structure of self-replication preceded the discovery of the structure of DNA.
achievement: He made fundamental contributions to mathematics, game theory, quantum mechanics, and computer architecture. His work in developing the von Neumann architecture paved the way for modern computing. Von Neumann's intellect and impact spanned multiple disciplines, leaving a lasting legacy. He formulated the von Neumann architecture upon which most modern computers are based.

name: Kathleen Booth
knownFor: Kathleen Hylda Valerie Booth was a British computer scientist and mathematician who wrote the first
intro: Kathleen Booth (1922-2020) was a British computer scientist and mathematician. She is known for her work on the development of assembly language and high-level programming languages. Booth played a key role in the design and implementation of the ARC (Automatic Relay Calculator), one of the earliest computers. Her contributions to computer science and programming languages have had a lasting impact on the field.
longBio: Kathleen Hylda Valerie Booth was a British computer scientist and mathematician who wrote the first assembly language and designed the assembler and autocode for the first computer systems at Birkbeck College, University of London. intro: Alan J. Perlis (1922-1990) was an influential American computer scientist known for his work in programming languages. He was the first recipient of the Turing Award for his contributions to the theory and practice of programming. Perlis' emphasis on clarity and simplicity in programming had a lasting impact on the field.
longBio: Alan Jay Perlis was an American computer scientist and professor at Purdue University, Carnegie Mellon University and Yale University. He is best known for his pioneering work in programming languages and in 1966 he became the first recipient of the ACM Turing Award.

Perlis was a strong advocate for rigorous programming techniques, modularity, and the importance of clear and expressive programming languages. He also played a crucial role in the development of ALGOL, an influential programming language. Perlis made significant contributions to the field of computer science through his research, teaching, and advocacy for the discipline.

name: Noam Chomsky
knownFor: Avram Noam Chomsky is an American intellectual, philosopher, linguist, political activist, and social critic
intro: Noam Chomsky is an influential American linguist, philosopher, cognitive scientist, and political activist. Known for his theories on generative grammar and the concept of universal grammar, Chomsky has made significant contributions to the study of language and has been a prominent critic of U.S. foreign policy and media propaganda.
longBio: Avram Noam Chomsky is an American intellectual, philosopher, linguist, political activist, and social critic. Sometimes called "the father of modern linguistics", Chomsky is also a major figure in analytic philosophy and one of the founders of the field of cognitive science. He is a laureate professor of linguistics at the University of Arizona and an institute professor emeritus at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT). Among the most cited living authors, Chomsky has written more than 150 books on topics such as linguistics, war, and politics. In addition to his work in linguistics, since the 1960s, Chomsky has been an influential voice on the American Left as a consistent critic of the foreign policy of the United States, contemporary capitalism, and corporatocracy.
achievement: Made contributions to computer science with his work in linguistics. Developed Chomsky hierarchy, directly impacting programming language theory and other branches of computer science.

database theory, in particular; and computer science, in general. His work was influential in distinguishing theoretical Computer Science from the disciplines of Mathematics and Electrical Engineering.

name: Al-Khwarizmi
knownFor: Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi, or simply al-Khwarizmi was a mathematician active during the Islamic Golden
intro: Al-Khwarizmi, a 9th-century Persian mathematician, is known as the "Father of Algebra." His book "Kitab al-Jabr wa al-Muqabala" introduced systematic methods for solving equations and laid the foundation for algebra. He also made contributions to trigonometry and the adoption of Arabic numerals, shaping the development of mathematics.
longBio: Muhammad ibn Musa al-Khwarizmi, or simply al-Khwarizmi was a mathematician active during the Islamic Golden Age, who produced Arabic-language works in mathematics, astronomy, and geography. Around 820, he worked at the House of Wisdom in Baghdad, the contemporary capital city of the Abbasid Caliphate. One of the most prominent scholars of the period, his works were widely influential on later authors, both in the Islamic world and Europe.
achievement: The term algorithm is derived from the algorism, the technique of performing arithmetic with Hindu–Arabic numerals popularised by al-Khwarizmi in his book On the Calculation with Hindu Numerals.

knownFor: Badīʿ az-Zaman Abu l-ʿIzz ibn Ismāʿīl ibn ar-Razāz al-Jazarī was a polymath, scholar, inventor, mechanical
intro: Ismail al-Jazari was a polymath who lived during the Islamic Golden Age (roughly 8th to 14th centuries). He was an inventor, mechanical engineer, artisan, artist and mathematician who wrote an influential book called "The Book of Knowledge of Ingenious Mechanical Devices" in which he described 100 mechanical devices, 80 of which were trick vessels of various kinds, along with instructions on how to construct them
longBio: Badīʿ az-Zaman Abu l-ʿIzz ibn Ismāʿīl ibn ar-Razāz al-Jazarī was a polymath, scholar, inventor, mechanical engineer, artisan and artist from the Artuqid Dynasty of Jazira in Mesopotamia. He is best known for writing The Book of Knowledge of Ingenious Mechanical Devices in 1206, where he described 50 mechanical devices, along with instructions on how to construct them. One of his more famous inventions is the elephant clock. He has been described as the "father of robotics" and modern day engineering.
achievement: He is best known for his book "The Book of Knowledge of Ingenious Mechanical Devices," also known as "Al-Jazari's Book of Knowledge." This book described various mechanical devices and automated systems, including clocks, water-raising machines, musical instruments, and more. Al-Jazari's work had a significant influence on the development of engineering and automation during the Islamic Golden Age.

name: Charles Sanders Peirce
knownFor: Charles Sanders Peirce was an American scientist, mathematician, logician, and philosopher who is sometimes known
intro: Charles Sanders Peirce was an American philosopher, logician, mathematician, and scientist. He is known for being one of the founders of pragmatism, an influential philosophical tradition, and for his work in logic, where he developed a form of predicate logic that's been essential to the development of various fields, including computer science. He also contributed to the development of semiotics, the study of signs and symbols.
longBio: Charles Sanders Peirce was an American scientist, mathematician, logician, and philosopher who is sometimes known as "the father of pragmatism". According to philosopher Paul Weiss, writing in 1934, Peirce was "the most original and versatile of America's philosophers and America's greatest logician". Bertrand Russell wrote in 1959, "he was one of the most original minds of the later nineteenth century and certainly the greatest American thinker ever".
achievement: Proved the functional completeness of the NOR gate. Proposed the implementation of logic via electrical circuits, decades before Claude Shannon. Extended Aristotelian logic with first-order predicate calculus, independently of Gottlob Frege, a crucial precursor in computability theory. Also relevant to early work on artificial intelligence, logic programming.

name: Herman Hollerith
knownFor: Herman Hollerith was a German-American statistician, inventor, and businessman who developed an electromechanical tabulating machine
intro: Herman Hollerith was an American inventor and businessman known for developing a mechanical tabulator based on punched cards to rapidly tabulate statistics from millions of pieces of data. This development was critical in the processing of data for the 1890 U.S. Census. Hollerith's company eventually became part of what is now IBM.
longBio: Herman Hollerith was a German-American statistician, inventor, and businessman who developed an electromechanical tabulating machine for punched cards to assist in summarizing information and, later, in accounting. His invention of the punched card tabulating machine, patented in 1884, marks the beginning of the era of mechanized binary code and semiautomatic data processing systems, and his concept dominated that landscape for nearly a century.
achievement: Widely regarded as the father of modern machine data processing, his invention of the punched card tabulating machine marked the beginning of the era of semiautomatic data processing systems

name: Kurt Gödel
knownFor: Kurt Friedrich Gödel was a logician, mathematician, and philosopher
intro: Kurt Gödel (1906-1978) was an Austrian mathematician and logician. He is best known for his incompleteness theorems, which had a profound impact on the foundations of mathematics. Gödel's theorems demonstrated that within any formal system, there are true statements that cannot be proven within that system. His work revolutionized the field of mathematical logic and had far-reaching implications for computer science and philosophy. Gödel made significant contributions to the understanding of logic, set theory, and the philosophy of mathematics. His intellectual legacy continues to influence and inspire mathematicians and scientists to this day.
longBio: Kurt Friedrich Gödel was a logician, mathematician, and philosopher. Considered along with Aristotle and Gottlob Frege to be one of the most significant logicians in history, Gödel profoundly influenced scientific and philosophical thinking in the 20th century, building on earlier work by Frege, Richard Dedekind, and Georg Cantor.
achievement: Proved that Peano arithmetic could not be both logically consistent and complete in first-order predicate calculus. Church, Kleene, and Turing developed the foundations of computation theory based on corollaries to Gödel's work.

name: Akira Nakashima
knownFor: Akira Nakashima was a Japanese electrical engineer of the NEC
intro: Akira Nakashima (1908-1970) was a Japanese electrical engineer known for his significant contributions to switching circuit theory. He obtained a bachelor's degree in electrical engineering from the Imperial University of Tokyo. Nakashima's papers published between 1934 and 1936 laid the foundations for digital circuit design, which had a profound impact on the development of digital computers and modern technology. His work in switching circuit theory is considered comparable to the achievements of Claude Shannon, who presented similar ideas during the same period.
longBio: Akira Nakashima was a Japanese electrical engineer of the NEC.
achievement: NEC engineer introduced switching circuit theory in papers from 1934 to 1936, laying the foundations for digital circuit design, in digital computers and other areas of modern technology.

name: John Atanasoff
knownFor: John Vincent Atanasoff was an American physicist and inventor credited with inventing the first electronic
intro: John Vincent Atanasoff (1903-1995) was an American physicist and inventor known for his work on the Atanasoff-Berry Computer (ABC). The ABC was the first electronic digital computer, incorporating key elements of modern computing. Atanasoff's contributions were instrumental in the development of computer technology and laid the groundwork for subsequent advancements.
longBio: John Vincent Atanasoff was an American physicist and inventor credited with inventing the first electronic digital computer. Atanasoff invented the first electronic digital computer in the 1930s at Iowa State College. Challenges to his claim were resolved in 1973 when the Honeywell v. Sperry Rand lawsuit ruled that Atanasoff was the inventor of the computer. His special-purpose machine has come to be called the Atanasoff–Berry Computer.
achievement: He is is credited with designing and building the first electronic digital computer. In the late 1930s, Atanasoff and his graduate student, Clifford Berry, developed the Atanasoff-Berry Computer (ABC), which used binary arithmetic and electronic switches to perform calculations. Although the ABC was not fully completed or widely recognized at the time, its design principles influenced subsequent computer development. Atanasoff's work made significant contributions to the advancement of computing technology.

name: Allen Coombs
knownFor: Allen William Mark ("Doc") Coombs was a British electronics engineer and early computer designer at
intro: Allen William Mark Coombs (1911-1995) was a British electronics engineer known for his work at the Post Office Research Station, Dollis Hill. He was a principal designer of the Mark II version of the Colossus computer, used for codebreaking at Bletchley Park during World War II. Coombs later worked on the MOSAIC machine and made contributions to optical character recognition. He frequently lectured on pattern recognition and was known for his facial tic.
longBio: Allen William Mark ("Doc") Coombs was a British electronics engineer and early computer designer at the Post Office Research Station, Dollis Hill, known for his work on the Colossus and MOSAIC (computer) computers.
achievement: Coombs headed the scientific side of R14, the division working on optical character recognition for postal mechanisation, which moved to the new BT Research Centre at Martlesham in Suffolk. His work on pattern recognition led to the development of an early postcode-reading machine.

name: Hedy Lamarr
knownFor: Hedy Lamarr was an Austrian and American actress and inventor
intro: Hedy Lamarr (1914-2000) was an Austrian-American actress and inventor. She co-invented frequency-hopping spread spectrum technology during World War II, which laid the foundation for modern wireless communication. Lamarr's contribution to telecommunications and her impact on technology have earned her recognition as a pioneering figure in the field.
longBio: Hedy Lamarr was an Austrian and American actress and inventor. Regarded as a successful film star, she also co-invented a radio guidance system during World War II.
achievement: At the beginning of World War II, she and composer George Antheil developed a radio guidance system for Allied torpedoes that used spread spectrum and frequency hopping technology to defeat the threat of jamming by the Axis powers. Although the US Navy did not adopt the technology until the 1960s, the principles of their work are incorporated into Bluetooth and GPS technology and are similar to methods used in legacy versions of CDMA and Wi-Fi. This work led to their induction into the National Inventors Hall of Fame in 2014.

name: Karen Spärck Jones
knownFor: Karen Ida Boalth Spärck Jones was a self-taught programmer and a pioneering British computer and
intro: Karen Spärck Jones (1935-2007) was a prominent British computer scientist known for her work in information retrieval and natural language processing. She developed the concept of inverse document frequency (IDF), a crucial component of modern search engines. Spärck Jones' contributions revolutionized information retrieval systems and computational linguistics.
longBio: Karen Ida Boalth Spärck Jones was a self-taught programmer and a pioneering British computer and information scientist responsible for the concept of inverse document frequency (IDF), a technology that underlies most modern search engines. She was an advocate for women in computer science, her slogan being, "Computing is too important to be left to men." In 2019, The New York Times published her belated obituary in its series Overlooked, calling her "a pioneer of computer science for work combining statistics and linguistics, and an advocate for women in the field." From 2008, to recognise her achievements in the fields of information retrieval (IR) and natural language processing (NLP), the Karen Spärck Jones Award is awarded annually to a recipient for outstanding research in one or both of her fields.
achievement: Renowned British computer scientist and pioneer in the field of natural language processing (NLP). Jones made significant contributions to information retrieval and computational linguistics, particularly in developing statistical methods for language modeling and text analysis. Her work laid the foundation for modern search engines and machine learning approaches in NLP, revolutionizing how computers understand and process human language.

name: John Backus
knownFor: John Warner Backus  was an American computer scientist
intro: John W. Backus (1924-2007) was an American computer scientist and inventor. He led the team at IBM that developed the programming language FORTRAN, one of the first high-level programming languages. Backus' work in language design and compiler development made programming more accessible and efficient, revolutionizing the field of software development.
longBio: John Warner Backus  was an American computer scientist. He led the team that invented and implemented FORTRAN, the first widely used high-level programming language, and was the inventor of the Backus–Naur form (BNF), a widely used notation to define syntaxes of formal languages. He also contributed to the design of ALGOL, and later researched the function-level programming paradigm, presenting his findings in his influential 1977 Turing Award lecture "Can Programming Be Liberated from the von Neumann Style?"

intro: Heinz Zemanek (1920-2014) was an Austrian computer scientist and pioneer in the field of computer technology. He led the development of the Mailüfterl computer, one of the earliest transistor-based computers. Zemanek also played a key role in the creation of the ALGOL programming language and made significant contributions to computer architecture and software engineering.
longBio: Heinz Zemanek was an Austrian computer pioneer who led the development, from 1954 to 1958, of one of the first complete transistorised computers on the European continent. The computer was nicknamed Mailüfterl — Viennese for "May breeze" — in reference to Whirlwind, a computer developed at MIT between 1945 and 1951.
achievement: Known for leading the development of Austria's first fully transistorized computer, the Mailüfterl. He also contributed to the design of the influential programming language ALGOL 60. Zemanek's work advanced computer technology and programming languages, leaving a lasting impact on the field.

name: Gottfried Leibniz
knownFor: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz was a German polymath active as a mathematician, philosopher, scientist, and diplomat
intro: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz (1646-1716) was a prominent German polymath, instrumental in the development of calculus, independently of Newton. His other areas of contribution encompass philosophy, politics, law, and theology. Leibniz's work on metaphysics and logic, particularly on monads, is still widely studied.
longBio: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz was a German polymath active as a mathematician, philosopher, scientist, and diplomat who is credited, alongside Isaac Newton, with the creation of calculus in addition to many other branches of mathematics, such as binary arithmetic and statistics. Leibniz has been called the "last universal genius" due to his vast expertise across fields, which became a rarity after his lifetime with the coming of the Industrial Revolution and the spread of specialized labour. He is a prominent figure in both the history of philosophy and the history of mathematics. He wrote works on philosophy, theology, ethics, politics, law, history, philology, games, music, and other studies. Leibniz also made major contributions to physics and technology, and anticipated notions that surfaced much later in probability theory, biology, medicine, geology, psychology, linguistics and computer science.
achievement: Made advances in symbolic logic, such as the Calculus ratiocinator, that were heavily influential on Gottlob Frege. Known for his contributions to mathematics, philosophy, and computer science. Co-inventor of calculus alongside Newton, his work laid foundational aspects of modern computing. Leibniz's philosophical theories significantly influenced subsequent Western thought.

name: George Boole
knownFor: George Boole was an English autodidact, mathematician, philosopher and logician who served as the first
intro: George Boole was an English mathematician, educator, philosopher and logician who lived in the 19th century. He worked in the fields of differential equations and algebraic logic, and is now best known as the author of "The Laws of Thought". This work contains Boolean algebra, which forms the basis of all modern computer arithmetic.
longBio: George Boole was an English autodidact, mathematician, philosopher and logician who served as the first professor of mathematics at Queen's College, Cork in Ireland. He worked in the fields of differential equations and algebraic logic, and is best known as the author of The Laws of Thought (1854), which contains Boolean algebra. Boolean logic, essential to computer programming, is credited with helping to lay the foundations for the Information Age.
achievement: Known for his foundational work in symbolic logic and Boolean algebra. Boole's algebraic system of logic became the basis for digital logic circuits and laid the groundwork for modern computer science and digital electronics. His Boolean algebra is fundamental in the design and analysis of computer algorithms and circuits, forming the backbone of digital computation. Boole's pioneering contributions have had a lasting impact on the field of computer science and the development of information technology.

name: Ada Lovelace

intro: Ada Lovelace, born as Augusta Ada Byron, was an English mathematician and writer known for her work on Charles Babbage's proposed mechanical general-purpose computer, the Analytical Engine. She is recognized for writing the world's first machine algorithm for an early computing machine that existed only on paper, thus often referred to as the world's first computer programmer.
longBio: Augusta Ada King, Countess of Lovelace, also known as Ada Lovelace, was an English mathematician and writer chiefly known for work on Charles Babbage's proposed mechanical general-purpose computer, the analytical engine. She was the first to recognise the machine had applications beyond pure calculation. Lovelace is often considered the first computer programmer.
achievement: An English mathematician and writer, chiefly known for her work on Charles Babbage's proposed mechanical general-purpose computer, the Analytical Engine. She was the first to recognize that the machine had applications beyond pure calculation, and created the first algorithm intended to be carried out by such a machine. As a result, she is often regarded as the first to recognize the full potential of a "computing machine" and the first computer programmer.

name: Emile Baudot
knownFor: Jean-Maurice-Émile Baudot was a French telegraph engineer and inventor of the first means of digital
intro: Émile Baudot was a French telegraph engineer and inventor of the Baudot code, which is used in telegraphy and early digital communications. The Baudot code was a binary 5-bit code, previously used in telegraphy and teletype machines, and is a predecessor to modern character encoding schemes like ASCII and Unicode.
longBio: Jean-Maurice-Émile Baudot was a French telegraph engineer and inventor of the first means of digital communication Baudot code. He was one of the pioneers of telecommunications. He invented a multiplexed printing telegraph system that used his code and allowed multiple transmissions over a single line. The baud unit was named after him.
achievement: Known for his invention of the Baudot code, a binary character encoding system used in early telegraphy and early versions of teleprinters. The Baudot code was a significant advancement in communication technology, as it allowed for the transmission of characters using a five-bit code, reducing the time and cost of telegraphy. Baudot's code laid the foundation for subsequent character encoding systems and played a crucial role in the development of modern digital communication. His contributions to telegraphy and information encoding have had a lasting impact on the field of telecommunications.

name: Konrad Zuse
knownFor: Konrad Ernst Otto Zuse was a German civil engineer, pioneering computer scientist, inventor and businessman
intro: Konrad Zuse (1910-1995) was a German engineer and computer scientist. He is credited with building the world's first fully functional program-controlled electromechanical computer, the Z3, in 1941. Zuse also developed the first high-level programming language, Plankalkül, and made significant contributions to the field of computer architecture and design.
longBio: Konrad Ernst Otto Zuse was a German civil engineer, pioneering computer scientist, inventor and businessman. His greatest achievement was the world's first programmable computer; the functional program-controlled Turing-complete Z3 became operational in May 1941. Thanks to this machine and its predecessors, Zuse is regarded by some as the inventor and father of the modern computer.
achievement: Built the first digital freely programmable computer, the Z1. Built the first functional program-controlled computer, the Z3 in 1941. The Z3 already used what later became known as Reverse Polish Notation, and it was proven to be Turing-complete in 1998. Produced the world's first commercial computer, the Z4. Designed the first high-level programming language, Plankalkül.

intro: Max Newman (1897-1984) was a British mathematician and computer scientist. He played a key role in the development of early computers, leading the design of the Colossus computer at Bletchley Park during World War II. Newman's contributions to computer science continued post-war, including establishing the Computing Machine Laboratory at the University of Manchester.
longBio: Maxwell Herman Alexander Newman, FRS, generally known as Max Newman, was a British mathematician and codebreaker. His work in World War II led to the construction of Colossus, the world's first operational, programmable electronic computer, and he established the Royal Society Computing Machine Laboratory at the University of Manchester, which produced the world's first working, stored-program electronic computer in 1948, the Manchester Baby.

name: J. Presper Eckert
knownFor: John Adam Presper "Pres" Eckert Jr
intro: J. Presper Eckert (1919-1995) was an American electrical engineer and computer pioneer. Along with John Mauchly, he co-designed and co-built the ENIAC, the world's first general-purpose electronic digital computer. Eckert's work was instrumental in the development of early computer technology and contributed to the advancement of modern computing.
longBio: John Adam Presper "Pres" Eckert Jr. was an American electrical engineer and computer pioneer. With John Mauchly, he designed the first general-purpose electronic digital computer (ENIAC), presented the first course in computing topics, founded the Eckert–Mauchly Computer Corporation, and designed the first commercial computer in the U.S., the UNIVAC, which incorporated Eckert's invention of the mercury delay-line memory.
achievement: Known for co-inventing the ENIAC (Electronic Numerical Integrator and Computer), one of the earliest general-purpose electronic computers. Eckert, along with John W. Mauchly, led the team that designed and built the ENIAC, which was completed in 1945. The ENIAC was a groundbreaking achievement that ushered in the era of electronic computing. Eckert's contributions to computer architecture and design continued with the development of the UNIVAC I, the first commercially available computer in the United States. Eckert's work revolutionized computing and laid the foundation for future technological advancements.

intro: Maurice Wilkes (1913-2010) was a British computer scientist and pioneer in the field of computing. He is best known for designing and building the EDSAC (Electronic Delay Storage Automatic Calculator), one of the world's first practical stored-program computers. Wilkes made significant contributions to computer architecture, programming languages, and operating systems. His work laid the foundation for modern computer systems and had a profound impact on the development of computing technology.
longBio: Sir Maurice Vincent Wilkes was an English computer scientist who designed and helped build the Electronic Delay Storage Automatic Calculator (EDSAC), one of the earliest stored-program computers, and who invented microprogramming, a method for using stored-program logic to operate the control unit of a central processing unit's circuits. In 1967 he won the ACM Turing Award. At the time of his death, Wilkes was an Emeritus Professor at the University of Cambridge.

name: Grace Hopper
knownFor: Grace Brewster Hopper was an American computer scientist, mathematician, and United States Navy rear admiral
intro: Grace Hopper (1906-1992) was a pioneering American computer scientist and naval officer. She developed the first compiler and played a crucial role in the development of early computer systems. Hopper's contributions to programming languages and software standardization have had a profound and lasting impact on the field of computer science.
longBio: Grace Brewster Hopper was an American computer scientist, mathematician, and United States Navy rear admiral. She was a pioneer of computer programming. Hopper was the first to devise the theory of machine-independent programming languages, and used this theory to develop the FLOW-MATIC programming language and COBOL, an early high-level programming language still in use today. She was also one of the first programmers on the Harvard Mark I computer. She is credited with writing the first computer manual, "A Manual of Operation for the Automatic Sequence Controlled Calculator."
achievement: Pioneered work on the necessity for high-level programming languages, which she termed automatic programming; wrote the A-O compiler, which heavily influenced the COBOL language

intro: Frank Gray, an American engineer and inventor, is known for his invention of the Gray code, a binary numeral system used in digital communication and information storage. The Gray code, also called reflected binary code, has applications in error detection, data transmission, and hardware systems.
longBio: Francis Tierney Gray is a Scottish football manager and former player. He played for Leeds United, Nottingham Forest, Sunderland and Darlington, while he also represented Scotland 32 times. He managed Darlington, Farnborough Town, Grays Athletic, Woking, Basingstoke Town and Bashley.
achievement: Physicist and researcher at Bell Labs, developed the reflected binary code (RBC) or Gray code.Gray's methodologies are used for error detection and correction in digital communication systems, such as QAM in digital subscriber line networks.

name: Herbert A. Simon
knownFor: Herbert Alexander Simon was an American scholar whose work influenced the fields of computer science,
intro: Herbert A. Simon (1916-2001) was an American economist, political scientist, and cognitive psychologist. He co-developed the General Problem Solver and, together with Allen Newell, pioneered the field of artificial intelligence. Simon's work in decision-making and problem-solving has had a lasting impact on cognitive science, economics, and computer science. He was awarded the Nobel Prize in Economics in 1978.
longBio: Herbert Alexander Simon was an American scholar whose work influenced the fields of computer science, economics, and cognitive psychology. His primary research interest was decision-making within organizations and he is best known for the theories of "bounded rationality" and "satisficing". He and Allen Newell received the ACM Turing Award in 1975, and he received the Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences in 1978.
achievement: He made significant contributions to multiple fields, including artificial intelligence, cognitive science, and decision-making. Simon is known for his research on human problem-solving and decision-making processes, and he developed the concept of bounded rationality, which recognizes the limitations of human decision-making in complex situations. He received the Turing Award in 1975 for his contributions to the field of artificial intelligence and cognitive psychology. Simon's interdisciplinary work has had a profound impact on our understanding of human cognition and behavior.

intro: Russell Gray Kirsch (1929-2020) was an American computer scientist and inventor. He is known for developing the first digital image scanner and the first digital image using a computer in 1957. Kirsch's contributions to digital imaging and his invention of the pixel have had a profound impact on the field of computer graphics and image processing.
longBio: Russell A. Kirsch was an American engineer at the National Bureau of Standards. He was recognized as the developer of the first digital image scanner, and subsequently scanned the world's first digital photograph – an image of his infant son.

knownFor: Ramon Llull, sometimes anglicized as Raymond Lully, was a Catholic philosopher, theologian, poet, missionary, apologist
intro: Ramon Llull (1232–1316), also known as Raymond Lull or Lully, was a mathematician, polymath, philosopher, logician, Franciscan tertiary, and writer from the Kingdom of Majorca. He is best known for his Ars Combinatoria, a method aimed at discovering truth by combining basic elements of knowledge through a systematic process.
longBio: Ramon Llull, sometimes anglicized as Raymond Lully, was a Catholic philosopher, theologian, poet, missionary, apologist and former knight, born in Palma de Majorca. He invented a philosophical system known as the Art, conceived as a type of universal logic to prove the truth of Christian doctrine to interlocutors of all faiths and nationalities. The Art consists of a set of general principles and combinatorial operations. It is illustrated with diagrams.

name: Joseph Marie Jacquard
knownFor: Joseph Marie Charles dit Jacquard was a French weaver and merchant
intro: Joseph Marie Jacquard was a French weaver and merchant who lived in the 18th and 19th centuries. He played a crucial role in the development of the earliest programmable loom, which is often referred to as the "Jacquard loom". This device used punched cards to control a sequence of operations. It was an important step in the history of computing hardware because it used a form of binary code and can be considered an early example of a stored-program machine.
longBio: Joseph Marie Charles dit Jacquard was a French weaver and merchant. He played an important role in the development of the earliest programmable loom, which in turn played an important role in the development of other programmable machines, such as an early version of digital compiler used by IBM to develop the modern day computer.
achievement: French inventor who created the Jacquard loom, a mechanical loom that used punched cards to control the weaving of complex patterns. This innovation revolutionized the textile industry and laid the foundation for modern computer programming and automation.

name: Gottlob Frege
knownFor: Friedrich Ludwig Gottlob Frege was a German philosopher, logician, and mathematician
intro: Gottlob Frege was a German philosopher, logician, and mathematician. He is considered one of the founders of modern logic and made significant contributions to the philosophy of language. Among his most notable works is the development of predicate logic, also known as "Fregean logic".
longBio: Friedrich Ludwig Gottlob Frege was a German philosopher, logician, and mathematician. He was a mathematics professor at the University of Jena, and is understood by many to be the father of analytic philosophy, concentrating on the philosophy of language, logic, and mathematics. Though he was largely ignored during his lifetime, Giuseppe Peano (1858–1932), Bertrand Russell (1872–1970), and, to some extent, Ludwig Wittgenstein (1889–1951) introduced his work to later generations of philosophers. Frege is widely considered to be one of the greatest logicians since Aristotle, and one of the most profound philosophers of mathematics ever.
achievement: Extended Aristotelian logic with first-order predicate calculus independently of Charles Sanders Peirce, a crucial precursor in computability theory; also relevant to early work on artificial intelligence, logic programming

name: Percy Ludgate
knownFor: Percy Edwin Ludgate was an Irish amateur scientist who designed the second analytical engine in
intro: Percy Edwin Ludgate was an Irish amateur scientist who is known for designing the second analytical engine, a general-purpose Turing-complete computer.
longBio: Percy Edwin Ludgate was an Irish amateur scientist who designed the second analytical engine in 1909, following the system of Charles Babbage in 1843. As part of the design, he devised a number of novel techniques, including Irish logarithms.
achievement: Charles Babbage in 1843 and Percy Ludgate in 1909 designed the first two Analytical Engines in history. Ludgate's engine used multiplication as its basis (using his own discrete Irish logarithms), had the first multiplier-accumulator (MAC), was first to exploit a MAC to perform division, stored numbers as displacements of rods in shuttles, and had several other novel features, including for program control.

name: Vannevar Bush
knownFor: Vannevar Bush was an American engineer, inventor and science administrator, who during World War II
intro: Vannevar Bush, was an American engineer and inventor who made important contributions to the field of computing. He is most known for his development of the Differential Analyzer, an analog computer that was used to solve differential equations. Additionally, he wrote a seminal essay, "As We May Think," which predicted many aspects of future technology, including hypertext, personal computers, the internet, and search engines. However, his contributions came later in the timeline of the development of computing technology, mainly in the mid-20th century.
longBio: Vannevar Bush was an American engineer, inventor and science administrator, who during World War II headed the U.S. Office of Scientific Research and Development (OSRD), through which almost all wartime military R&D was carried out, including important developments in radar and the initiation and early administration of the Manhattan Project. He emphasized the importance of scientific research to national security and economic well-being, and was chiefly responsible for the movement that led to the creation of the National Science Foundation.
achievement: Developed the concept of the memex, a hypothetical machine that anticipated the World Wide Web, and proposed the idea of a collective knowledge base, inspiring future information retrieval and storage systems. His visionary work laid the foundation for modern hypertext and information science.

name: Stephen Cole Kleene
knownFor: Stephen Cole Kleene was an American mathematician and logician
intro: Stephen Cole Kleene (1909-1994) was an American mathematician and logician. He made significant contributions to the field of mathematical logic, particularly in the areas of recursion theory and computability. Kleene developed the concept of regular expressions and formalized the notion of recursive functions. His work played a crucial role in the development of theoretical computer science and laid the foundations for modern computability theory. Kleene's research and writings continue to be influential in the field to this day.
longBio: Stephen Cole Kleene was an American mathematician and logician. One of the students of Alonzo Church, Kleene, along with Rózsa Péter, Alan Turing, Emil Post, and others, is best known as a founder of the branch of mathematical logic known as recursion theory, which subsequently helped to provide the foundations of theoretical computer science. Kleene's work grounds the study of computable functions. A number of mathematical concepts are named after him: Kleene hierarchy, Kleene algebra, the Kleene star, Kleene's recursion theorem and the Kleene fixed-point theorem. He also invented regular expressions in 1951 to describe McCulloch-Pitts neural networks, and made significant contributions to the foundations of mathematical intuitionism.
achievement: Pioneered work with Alonzo Church on the Lambda Calculus that first laid down the foundations of computation theory.

intro: Arthur Walter Burks (1915-2008) was an American mathematician and computer scientist. Burks also made significant contributions to the field of symbolic logic and the design of early computer architectures.
longBio: Arthur Walter Burks was an American mathematician who worked in the 1940s as a senior engineer on the project that contributed to the design of the ENIAC, the first general-purpose electronic digital computer. Burks was also for several decades a faculty member at the University of Michigan in Ann Arbor.

name: Allen Newell
knownFor: Allen Newell was an American researcher in computer science and cognitive psychology at the RAND
intro: Allen Newell (1927-1992) was an American computer scientist and cognitive psychologist. He co-developed the Logic Theorist, the first artificial intelligence program, and later worked on the development of the General Problem Solver. Newell also made significant contributions to cognitive psychology, particularly in the field of human problem-solving and decision-making.
longBio: Allen Newell was an American researcher in computer science and cognitive psychology at the RAND Corporation and at Carnegie Mellon University's School of Computer Science, Tepper School of Business, and Department of Psychology. He, Herbert A. Simon, and Cliff Shaw contributed to the Information Processing Language (1956) and two of the earliest AI programs, the Logic Theorist (1956) and the General Problem Solver (1957). He and Simon were awarded the ACM Turing Award in 1975 for their contributions to artificial intelligence and the psychology of human cognition.
achievement: He co-developed the Logic Theorist and General Problem Solver, early AI programs. Newell's research on symbolic processing and problem-solving algorithms laid the foundation for AI. His contributions have had a lasting impact on the field of computer science.

name: Peter Naur
knownFor: Peter Naur was a Danish computer science pioneer and 2005 Turing Award winner
intro: Peter Naur (1928-2016) was a Danish computer scientist and Turing Award recipient. He made significant contributions to the development of programming languages, particularly with the development of the programming language ALGOL 60. Naur's work emphasized the importance of precise language specification and formal methods in software development.
longBio: Peter Naur was a Danish computer science pioneer and 2005 Turing Award winner. He is best remembered as a contributor, with John Backus, to the Backus–Naur form (BNF) notation used in describing the syntax for most programming languages. He also contributed to creating the language ALGOL 60.
achievement: Co-developed the Backus-Naur Form (BNF) used for describing programming language syntax. He contributed to the development of Algol 60, a major influence on programming languages. Naur was also a pioneer in software engineering and received the Turing Award in 2005 for his contributions.

name: J. C. R. Licklider
knownFor: Joseph Carl Robnett Licklider, known simply as J
intro: J.C.R. Licklider (1915-1990) was an American psychologist and computer scientist. He is considered one of the pioneers of the field of computer science. Licklider was instrumental in the development of ARPANET, the precursor to the internet, and he played a key role in promoting the concept of human-computer interaction and the idea of a global computer network. His visionary ideas laid the foundation for the digital age we live in today.
longBio: Joseph Carl Robnett Licklider, known simply as J. C. R. or "Lick", was an American psychologist and computer scientist who is considered to be among the most prominent figures in computer science development and general computing history.
achievement: Known as "Lick," was an American psychologist and computer scientist who played a significant role in the early development of the Internet and human-computer interaction. He is often referred to as one of the "fathers of the Internet." Licklider's ideas and vision about interactive computing and a connected global network laid the foundation for what would become the modern Internet.

name: Leonard Kleinrock
knownFor: Leonard Kleinrock is an American computer scientist and Internet pioneer
intro: Leonard Kleinrock (born 1934) is an American computer scientist known for his pioneering work on packet switching theory, which laid the foundation for the development of the Internet. He played a key role in the creation of ARPANET, the precursor to the Internet, and is considered one of the fathers of the Internet.
longBio: Leonard Kleinrock is an American computer scientist and Internet pioneer. He is Distinguished Professor Emeritus of Computer Science at UCLA's Henry Samueli School of Engineering and Applied Science. Kleinrock made several important contributions to the field of computer science, in particular to the mathematical foundations of data communication in computer networking. He has received numerous prestigious awards.
achievement: Pioneered the application of queueing theory to model delays in message switching networks in his Ph.D. thesis in 1961–1962, published as a book in 1964. He later published several of the standard works on the subject. In the early 1970s, he applied queueing theory to model the performance of packet switching networks. This work played an influential role in the development of the ARPANET, the precursor to the Internet. He supervised many graduate students who worked on the communication protocols for internetworking which led to the Internet. His theoretical work on hierarchical routing in the late 1970s with student Farouk Kamoun remains critical to the operation of the Internet today.

name: Douglas Engelbart
knownFor: Douglas Carl Engelbart was an American engineer, inventor, and a pioneer in many aspects of
intro: Douglas Engelbart (1925-2013) was an American computer scientist and inventor. He is best known for inventing the computer mouse and pioneering several key concepts in human-computer interaction. Engelbart's groundbreaking work at the Stanford Research Institute contributed to the development of graphical user interfaces, hypertext, and collaborative tools, shaping the future of computing.
longBio: Douglas Carl Engelbart was an American engineer, inventor, and a pioneer in many aspects of computer science. He is best known for his work on founding the field of human–computer interaction, particularly while at his Augmentation Research Center Lab in SRI International, which resulted in creation of the computer mouse, and the development of hypertext, networked computers, and precursors to graphical user interfaces. These were demonstrated at The Mother of All Demos in 1968. Engelbart's law, the observation that the intrinsic rate of human performance is exponential, is named after him.

name: Marvin Minsky
knownFor: Marvin Minsky was an American mathematician who did research in cognitive and computer science aspects
intro: Marvin Minsky (1927-2016) was an American cognitive scientist and computer scientist. He co-founded the field of artificial intelligence (AI) and made significant contributions to the development of AI, cognitive psychology, and robotics. Minsky's work on neural networks, perception, and machine learning has had a profound impact on the field of AI.
longBio: Marvin Minsky was an American mathematician who did research in cognitive and computer science aspects of artificial intelligence (AI). After three years as a Junior Fellow of the Harvard Society of Fellows, Minsky joined the faculty at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) in 1958 and spent the rest of his career at that institution. There, he co-founded MIT's AI laboratory, among other initiatives, and wrote extensively about AI and philosophy. He, computer scientist John McCarthy, and others have been called the "fathers of AI". At the time he was made emeritus, Minsky was the Toshiba Professor of Media Art & Sciences at MIT.
achievement: Co-founder of Artificial Intelligence Lab at Massachusetts Institute of Technology, author of several texts on AI and philosophy. Critic of the perceptron.

name: Seymour Cray
knownFor: Seymour Roger Cray was an American electrical engineer, computer scientist, mathematician, and supercomputer architect who
intro: Seymour Cray (1925-1996) was an American electrical engineer and computer architect. He is considered one of the pioneers in supercomputer design. Cray founded Cray Research and developed a series of supercomputers, including the Cray-1 and Cray-2, which set new standards for computing power and speed. His innovations greatly advanced the field of high-performance computing.
longBio: Seymour Roger Cray was an American electrical engineer, computer scientist, mathematician, and supercomputer architect who designed a series of computers that were the fastest in the world for decades, and founded Cray Research, which built many of these machines. Called "the father of supercomputing", Cray has been credited with creating the supercomputer industry. Joel S. Birnbaum, then chief technology officer of Hewlett-Packard, said of him: "It seems impossible to exaggerate the effect he had on the industry; many of the things that high performance computers now do routinely were at the farthest edge of credibility when Seymour envisioned them." Larry Smarr, then director of the National Center for Supercomputing Applications at the University of Illinois said that Cray is "the Thomas Edison of the supercomputing industry."

name: Ole-Johan Dahl

intro: Ole-Johan Dahl (1931-2002) was a Norwegian computer scientist and co-creator of the programming language Simula. He made significant contributions to object-oriented programming and software engineering. Dahl's work on Simula, the first language to support object-oriented programming concepts, had a profound impact on the development of modern programming languages and software engineering practices.
longBio: Ole-Johan Dahl was a Norwegian computer scientist. Dahl was a professor of computer science at the University of Oslo and is considered to be one of the fathers of Simula and object-oriented programming along with Kristen Nygaard. In 2001, Dahl and Nygaard won the ACM Turing Award.
achievement: With Kristen Nygaard, co-developed the Simula programming language in the 1960s, introducing object-oriented programming concepts. Simula's influence on modern languages earned Dahl the Turing Award in 2001. He also contributed to computer science education as a professor at the University of Oslo.

intro: Betty Holberton (1917-2001) was an American computer scientist and one of the original programmers of the ENIAC, one of the first general-purpose electronic computers. She made significant contributions to the development of computer programming and software engineering, including the creation of the first software application for the UNIVAC I computer. Holberton's work paved the way for the advancement of computing technology.
longBio: Frances Elizabeth Holberton was an American computer scientist who was one of the six original programmers of the first general-purpose electronic digital computer, ENIAC. The other five ENIAC programmers were Jean Bartik, Ruth Teitelbaum, Kathleen Antonelli, Marlyn Meltzer, and Frances Spence.

name: Ivan Sutherland
knownFor: Ivan Edward Sutherland is an American computer scientist and Internet pioneer, widely regarded as a
intro: Ivan Sutherland (born 1938) is an American computer scientist and pioneer in the field of computer graphics. He is known for his groundbreaking work on Sketchpad, the first graphical user interface, and for his development of the "Sutherland-Hodgman" algorithm for polygon clipping. Sutherland's contributions have had a profound impact on computer graphics and human-computer interaction.
longBio: Ivan Edward Sutherland is an American computer scientist and Internet pioneer, widely regarded as a pioneer of computer graphics. His early work in computer graphics as well as his teaching with David C. Evans in that subject at the University of Utah in the 1970s was pioneering in the field. Sutherland, Evans, and their students from that era developed several foundations of modern computer graphics. He received the 1988 ACM Turing Award for the invention of the Sketchpad, an early predecessor to the sort of graphical user interface that has become ubiquitous in personal computers, and his contributions to computer graphics. He is a member of the National Academy of Engineering, as well as the National Academy of Sciences among many other major awards. In 2012, he was awarded the Kyoto Prize in Advanced Technology for "pioneering achievements in the development of computer graphics and interactive interfaces".
achievement: Known for his groundbreaking work in computer graphics and interactive technologies. Sutherland is credited with inventing Sketchpad, an early computer-aided design (CAD) system that allowed users to interact with graphical objects using a light pen. His work laid the foundation for many subsequent developments in computer graphics, including the development of 3D modeling and rendering techniques. Sutherland also made significant contributions to virtual reality, human-computer interaction, and computer-aided surgery. He received the Turing Award in 1988 for his pioneering contributions to computer graphics. Sutherland's work has had a lasting impact on the field of computer science and has influenced generations of researchers and practitioners.

name: Federico Faggin
knownFor: Federico Faggin is an Italian-American physicist, engineer, inventor and entrepreneur
intro: Federico Faggin (born 1941) is an Italian-American engineer and inventor. He is known for his role in the development of the first commercial microprocessor, the Intel 4004, and for his work on the MOS Technology 6502 microprocessor. Faggin's contributions have played a pivotal role in the advancement of computer technology.
longBio: Federico Faggin is an Italian-American physicist, engineer, inventor and entrepreneur. He is best known for designing the first commercial microprocessor, the Intel 4004. He led the 4004 (MCS-4) project and the design group during the first five years of Intel's microprocessor effort. Faggin also created, while working at Fairchild Semiconductor in 1968, the self-aligned MOS (metal–oxide–semiconductor) silicon-gate technology (SGT), which made possible MOS semiconductor memory chips, CCD image sensors, and the microprocessor. After the 4004, he led development of the Intel 8008 and 8080, using his SGT methodology for random logic chip design, which was essential to the creation of early Intel microprocessors. He was co-founder and CEO of Zilog, the first company solely dedicated to microprocessors, and led the development of the Zilog Z80 and Z8 processors. He was later the co-founder and CEO of Cygnet Technologies, and then Synaptics.
achievement: Known for designing the world's first commercially available microprocessor, the Intel 4004. He co-founded Zilog and developed the popular Z80 microprocessor. Faggin's contributions revolutionized the field of computing and semiconductor technology, shaping the modern digital age.

name: Richard Stallman
knownFor: Richard Matthew Stallman, also known by his initials rms, is an American free software movement
intro: Richard Stallman (born 1953) is an American software developer and free software advocate. He founded the Free Software Foundation and initiated the GNU Project, aiming to create a free and open-source operating system. Stallman has been instrumental in promoting the principles of software freedom, open collaboration, and user autonomy in the digital world.
longBio: Richard Matthew Stallman, also known by his initials rms, is an American free software movement activist and programmer. He campaigns for software to be distributed in such a manner that its users have the freedom to use, study, distribute, and modify that software. Software which ensures these freedoms is termed free software. Stallman launched the GNU Project, founded the Free Software Foundation (FSF) in October 1985, developed the GNU C Compiler and GNU Emacs, and wrote all versions of the GNU General Public License.
achievement: Stallman launched the GNU Project in September 1983 to create a Unix-like computer operating system composed entirely of free software. With this, he also launched the free software movement.

name: John W. Tukey
knownFor: John Wilder Tukey was an American mathematician and statistician, best known for the development of
intro: John W. Tukey (1915-2000) was an American mathematician and statistician. He made significant contributions to the fields of exploratory data analysis, fast Fourier transform (FFT), and statistical computing. Tukey's work revolutionized data analysis methods and statistical thinking, and he coined the term "bit" in computing. He received numerous honors for his contributions.
longBio: John Wilder Tukey was an American mathematician and statistician, best known for the development of the fast Fourier Transform (FFT) algorithm, the box plot and for laying the foundations of the field of exploratory data analysis. The Tukey range test, the Tukey lambda distribution, the Tukey test of additivity, and the Teichmüller–Tukey lemma all bear his name. He is also credited with coining the term bit and the first published use of the word software.
achievement: Known for his significant contributions to exploratory data analysis and statistical computing. Tukey developed innovative statistical techniques, such as the box plot and the fast Fourier transform algorithm, which have become widely used in data analysis and signal processing. His work emphasized the importance of visualizing and understanding data through graphical methods.

name: Ken Thompson
knownFor: Kenneth Lane Thompson is an American pioneer of computer science
intro: Ken Thompson (born 1943) is an American computer scientist and co-creator of the Unix operating system and the C programming language. He played a key role in the development of the original Unix system, including the creation of important tools and utilities. Thompson's work has had a lasting impact on the field of operating systems and software development.
longBio: Kenneth Lane Thompson is an American pioneer of computer science. Thompson worked at Bell Labs for most of his career where he designed and implemented the original Unix operating system. He also invented the B programming language, the direct predecessor to the C language, and was one of the creators and early developers of the Plan 9 operating system. Other notable contributions included his work on regular expressions and early computer text editors QED and ed, the definition of the UTF-8 encoding, and his work on computer chess that included the creation of endgame tablebases and the chess machine Belle.
achievement: Created the Unix operating system, the B programming language, Plan 9 operating system, the first machine to achieve a Master rating in chess, and the UTF-8 encoding at Bell Labs and the Go programming language at Google.

intro: Elizabeth Feinler is an American computer scientist and pioneer in the field of internet technology. She played a key role in the development of the ARPANET, the precursor to the internet, and was instrumental in establishing the first internet address registry. Feinler's work has had a lasting impact on the growth and organization of the internet.
longBio: Elizabeth Jocelyn "Jake"  Feinler is an American information scientist. From 1972 until 1989 she was director of the Network Information Systems Center at the Stanford Research Institute. Her group operated the Network Information Center (NIC) for the ARPANET as it evolved into the Defense Data Network (DDN) and the Internet. While at NIC, her and her team created the Host Naming Registry (WHOIS) and the top-level domain naming convention that the internet still uses today. Before her inventions the internet would have not been able to scale and grow as much as it has today. Without her contributions users would have constantly use their IP address to get to their desired website, with the top-level naming convention it has made the navigation much more user friendly and convenient. The impact that her contribution has made is to make the internet much more usable for the average person and much easier for the internet to be managed as each domain there was a person who was attached to it, which is what the Host Naming Registry was for. Her contributions to the internet are still being used despite her retirement and is now managed by ICANN.
achievement: Lead team that defined a simple text file format for Internet host names, which became the Domain Name System; her group became the naming authority for the top-level domains of .mil, .gov, .edu, .org, and .com

name: Frances E. Allen
knownFor: Frances Elizabeth Allen was an American computer scientist and pioneer in the field of optimizing
intro: Frances E. Allen (1932-2020) was an American computer scientist and pioneer in the field of compiler optimization. She became the first woman to receive the Turing Award for her fundamental contributions to the theory and practice of optimizing compiler techniques. Allen's work significantly advanced the field of programming languages and compiler design.
longBio: Frances Elizabeth Allen was an American computer scientist and pioneer in the field of optimizing compilers. Allen was the first woman to become an IBM Fellow, and in 2006 became the first woman to win the Turing Award. Her achievements include seminal work in compilers, program optimization, and parallelization. She worked for IBM from 1957 to 2002 and subsequently was a Fellow Emerita.
achievement: American computer scientist and pioneer in the field of compiler optimization. She was the first woman to receive the Turing Award in 2006 for her groundbreaking contributions to the theory and practice of optimizing compiler techniques. Allen's work significantly improved the performance of computer programs by developing advanced optimization algorithms and methodologies. Her research laid the foundation for modern compiler design and had a profound impact on the field of computer science. Allen was also an advocate for diversity and inclusion in computing, actively promoting the participation of women in the field.
