name: Whitfield Diffie
knownFor: Bailey Whitfield 'Whit' Diffie ForMemRS is an American cryptographer and mathematician and one of the
intro: Whitfield Diffie (born 1944) is an American cryptographer and one of the pioneers of public-key cryptography. He, along with Martin Hellman, introduced the concept of asymmetric encryption, which revolutionized secure communication over the internet. Diffie's work laid the foundation for modern cryptographic protocols and played a crucial role in ensuring digital privacy and security.
longBio: Bailey Whitfield 'Whit' Diffie ForMemRS is an American cryptographer and mathematician and one of the pioneers of public-key cryptography along with Martin Hellman and Ralph Merkle. Diffie and Hellman's 1976 paper New Directions in Cryptography introduced a radically new method of distributing cryptographic keys, that helped solve key distribution—a fundamental problem in cryptography. Their technique became known as Diffie–Hellman key exchange. The article stimulated the almost immediate public development of a new class of encryption algorithms, the asymmetric key algorithms.
achievement: Fundamental contributions to modern cryptography. Diffie and Hellman's groundbreaking 1976 paper "New Directions in Cryptography introduced the ideas of public-key cryptography and digital signatures, the foundation of security protocols used on the Internet today.

name: Gary Kildall
knownFor: Gary Arlen Kildall was an American computer scientist and microcomputer entrepreneur
intro: Gary Kildall (1942-1994) was an American computer scientist and entrepreneur. He developed the CP/M operating system, which became one of the most popular operating systems for early microcomputers. Kildall's work laid the foundation for modern operating systems, and he played a key role in the early development of the personal computer industry.
longBio: Gary Arlen Kildall was an American computer scientist and microcomputer entrepreneur. During the 1970s, Kildall created the operating system CP/M among other operating systems and programming tools, and subsequently founded Digital Research, Inc. to market and sell his software products. He is considered a pioneer of the personal computer revolution.
achievement: Introduced the theory of data-flow analysis in optimizing compilers (global expression optimization, Kildall's method). Worked on instruction set emulators (INTERP), found an innovative software relocation method (page boundary relocation), and laid the foundation to the concepts of binary recompilation (XLT86). Developed the first high-level programming language and compiler for microcomputers (PL/M) and the first mainstream operating system for microcomputers (CP/M). Invented the concept of a hardware abstraction layer called the BIOS, with both conceptually laying the foundation to all DOS-based operating systems on personal computers. Worked on diskette track buffering schemes, read-ahead algorithms, virtual disk drives, and file system caching. Developed the first computer interface for video disks and pioneered CD-ROM file systems, introducing the first encyclopedia for computers (The Electronic Encyclopedia). Pioneered a modular PBX communication system integrating land-lines with mobile phones (Intelliphone) and to remotely connect with home appliances.

knownFor: François Gernelle is a French engineer, computer scientist and entrepreneur famous for inventing the first
intro: François Gernelle is a French inventor and computer engineer known for his contributions to the development of the Micral N, one of the world's first commercial microcomputers. Alongside André Trương Trọng Thi, Gernelle co-created the Micral N in 1973, which utilized an Intel 8008 processor and played a significant role in the advancement of personal computing.
longBio: François Gernelle is a French engineer, computer scientist and entrepreneur famous for inventing the first micro-computer using a micro-processor, the Micral N.
achievement: He is renowned for inventing the Micral N, the first micro-computer using a micro-processor. Gernelle's career includes working at Intertechnique and R2E, where he designed the Micral N and several other microcomputers. He later joined Bull Micral before founding his own company, FORUM International, focused on creating professional computers powered by Prologue OS. Gernelle's contributions have played a significant role in the development of microcomputers and computer systems.

knownFor: Charles William Bachman III was an American computer scientist, who spent his entire career as
intro: Charles Bachman (born 1924) is an American computer scientist and pioneer in the field of database management systems. He developed the Integrated Data Store (IDS), an influential network model database management system. Bachman's work on data structures and database design has greatly influenced the field of data management and laid the foundation for modern database systems.
longBio:
Charles William Bachman III was an American computer scientist, who spent his entire career as an industrial researcher, developer, and manager rather than in academia. He was particularly known for his work in the early development of database management systems.
His techniques of layered architecture include his namesake Bachman diagrams. He won the 1973 ACM Turing Award.
achievement: Renowned for his work in database management systems. He developed the Integrated Data Store (IDS), introduced the Bachman Diagram and Data Structure Diagram (DSD), and received the Turing Award for his significant contributions to the field.

name: Robert W. Floyd
knownFor: Robert W
intro: Robert W. Floyd (1936-2001) was an American computer scientist and mathematician. He made significant contributions to the field of algorithm analysis and design, particularly in the areas of graph algorithms and program verification. Floyd is known for his work on algorithmic complexity and the development of the Floyd-Warshall algorithm for all-pairs shortest paths.
longBio: Robert W. Floyd was an American computer scientist. His contributions include the design of the Floyd–Warshall algorithm, which efficiently finds all shortest paths in a graph and his work on parsing; Floyd's cycle-finding algorithm for detecting cycles in a sequence was attributed to him as well. In one isolated paper he introduced the important concept of error diffusion for rendering images, also called Floyd–Steinberg dithering. He pioneered in the field of program verification using logical assertions with the 1967 paper Assigning Meanings to Programs. This was a contribution to what later became Hoare logic. Floyd received the Turing Award in 1978.
achievement: Had a clear influence on methodologies for the creation of efficient and reliable software; helped to found these important sub-fields of computer science: theory of parsing, semantics of programming languages, automatic program verification, automatic program synthesis, and analysis of algorithms.

name: Gerard Salton
knownFor: Gerard A
intro: Gerard Salton (1927-1995) was an American computer scientist and pioneer in the field of information retrieval. He developed the Vector Space Model, a foundational concept in modern information retrieval systems. Salton's work revolutionized the field and laid the groundwork for techniques used in search engines and text mining applications today.
longBio: Gerard A. "Gerry" Salton was a professor of computer science at Cornell University. Salton was perhaps the leading computer scientist working in the field of information retrieval during his time, and "the father of Information Retrieval". His group at Cornell developed the SMART Information Retrieval System, which he initiated when he was at Harvard. It was the first system to use the now popular vector space model for information retrieval.
achievement: Distinguished computer scientist and information retrieval specialist. Salton is widely regarded as the father of modern information retrieval. His pioneering work led to the development of the vector space model, which revolutionized document ranking and retrieval. Salton's contributions in the field of information retrieval have had a profound impact on search engines, text mining, and text analysis, shaping the way we access and utilize information in the digital age.

intro: Bjarne Stroustrup (born 1950) is a Danish computer scientist known for creating the C++ programming language. He has contributed significantly to the field of programming languages, particularly in the areas of object-oriented programming and generic programming. Stroustrup's work has had a profound impact on software development and remains widely influential in the industry.
longBio: Bjarne Stroustrup is a Danish computer scientist, known for the development of the C++ programming language. He led the Large-scale Programming Research department at Bell Labs, served as a professor of computer science at Texas A&M University, and spent over a decade at Morgan Stanley while also being a visiting professor at Columbia University. Since 2022 he has been a full professor at Columbia.
achievement: Creator of the C++ programming language. His work on C++ introduced object-oriented programming features and made it widely used in industry. Stroustrup's contributions and advocacy have solidified C++ as a powerful and influential language in software development.

name: Philip Rubin
knownFor: Philip E
intro: Philip Rubin is an American cognitive scientist and neuroscientist. He has made significant contributions to the study of auditory perception, speech recognition, and the neurobiology of language. Rubin has served in various leadership roles in academia and research organizations, working to advance our understanding of the human brain and cognition.
longBio:
Philip E. Rubin is an American cognitive scientist, technologist, and science administrator known for raising the visibility of behavioral and cognitive science, neuroscience, and ethical issues related to science, technology, and medicine, at a national level. 
His research career is noted for his theoretical contributions and pioneering technological developments, starting in the 1970s, related to speech synthesis and speech production, including articulatory synthesis and sinewave synthesis, and their use in studying complex temporal events, particularly understanding the biological bases of speech and language.
achievement: Developed pioneering computational speech synthesis systems for use in the experimental study of speech perception and production, including articulatory synthesis and sinewave synthesis. Also designed the HADES signal processing system, a predecessor of MATLAB.

name: Edmund M. Clarke
knownFor: Edmund Melson Clarke, Jr
intro: Edmund M. Clarke (1945-2020) was an American computer scientist and pioneer in the field of formal verification. He developed the symbolic model checking technique, which revolutionized the verification of hardware and software systems. Clarke's work has had a profound impact on the advancement of formal methods and the reliability of complex computing systems.
longBio: Edmund Melson Clarke, Jr. was an American computer scientist and academic noted for developing model checking, a method for formally verifying hardware and software designs. He was the FORE Systems Professor of Computer Science at Carnegie Mellon University. Clarke, along with E. Allen Emerson and Joseph Sifakis, received the 2007 ACM Turing Award.
achievement: Renowned computer scientist and a pioneer in the field of formal verification. Clarke is widely known for his co-invention of model checking, a technique for verifying hardware and software systems. His seminal work, including the influential paper "Automatic Verification of Finite-State Concurrent Systems Using Temporal Logic Specifications," has laid the foundation for the development and advancement of model checking. Clarke's contributions have revolutionized the field of formal methods and played a crucial role in ensuring the correctness and reliability of complex computer systems.

name: E. Allen Emerson
knownFor: Ernest Allen Emerson II was an American computer scientist and winner of the 2007 Turing
intro: E. Allen Emerson (born 1954) is an American computer scientist and pioneer in the field of formal verification. He co-developed the symbolic model checking technique, which has had a transformative impact on verifying hardware and software systems for correctness. Emerson's work has been instrumental in advancing the field of formal methods in computer science.
longBio: Ernest Allen Emerson II was an American computer scientist and winner of the 2007 Turing Award. He was a professor at the University of Texas at Austin from 1981 to 2016.
achievement: Known for his work in formal methods, particularly in establishing program correctness. Inspired by papers and talks by Tony Hoare and Zohar Manna, Emerson co-invented and co-developed model checking, an algorithmic approach to verifying finite-state concurrent programs. His seminal paper with Ed Clarke in 1981 laid the foundations for the field of model checking. Emerson's contributions include advancements in model checking algorithms, abstractions, reductions, decision procedures, and program synthesis.

longBio: John Edward Hopcroft is an American theoretical computer scientist. His textbooks on theory of computation and data structures are regarded as standards in their fields. He is a professor emeritus at Cornell University, co-director of the Center on Frontiers of Computing Studies at Peking University, the director of the John Hopcroft Center for Computer Science at Shanghai Jiao Tong University and Hopcroft Center on Computer Science at Huazhong University of Science and Technology. He and Robert Tarjan won the 1986 ACM Turing Award.
achievement: Renowned computer scientist, has made significant contributions to algorithms and formal languages. He co-authored the influential textbook "Introduction to Automata Theory, Languages, and Computation." He received the Turing Award for compiler optimization contributions. His work has advanced computer science and inspired generations of researchers.

intro: Raj Reddy (born 1937) is an Indian-American computer scientist and recipient of the Turing Award. Reddy's work on voice recognition systems and language understanding has had a profound impact on the development of intelligent systems and human-computer interaction.
longBio: Dabbala Rajagopal "Raj" Reddy is an Indian-born American computer scientist. He is one of the early pioneers of artificial intelligence and has served on the faculty of Stanford and Carnegie Mellon for over 50 years. He was the founding director of the Robotics Institute at Carnegie Mellon University. He was instrumental in helping to create Rajiv Gandhi University of Knowledge Technologies in India, to cater to the educational needs of the low-income, gifted, rural youth. He was the founding chairman of International Institute of Information Technology, Hyderabad. He and Edward Feigenbaum won the 1994 ACM Turing Award, sometimes known as the Nobel Prize of computer science, for their work in the field of artificial intelligence. Reddy was the first person of Asian origin to receive the Turing Award.
achievement: Pioneering the design and construction of large scale artificial intelligence systems, demonstrating the practical importance and potential commercial impact of artificial intelligence technology.

intro: Judea Pearl (born 1936) is an Israeli-American computer scientist and recipient of the Turing Award. He has made significant contributions to the field of artificial intelligence, particularly in the area of probabilistic reasoning and causal inference. Pearl's work on Bayesian networks and causal models has advanced the understanding of complex systems and decision-making processes.
longBio: Judea Pearl is an Israeli-American electrical engineer, computer scientist and philosopher, best known for championing the probabilistic approach to artificial intelligence and the development of Bayesian networks. He is also credited for developing a theory of causal and counterfactual inference based on structural models. In 2011, the Association for Computing Machinery (ACM) awarded Pearl with the Turing Award, the highest distinction in computer science, "for fundamental contributions to artificial intelligence through the development of a calculus for probabilistic and causal reasoning". He is the author of several books, including the technical Causality: Models, Reasoning and Inference, and The Book of Why, a book on causality aimed at the general public.

intro: C. A. R. Hoare (born 1934), also known as Tony Hoare, is a British computer scientist and Turing Award recipient. Hoare's work has significantly influenced the field of computer science and software engineering.
longBio: Sir Charles Antony Richard Hoare, known as Tony Hoare or C. A. R. name: James Cooley
knownFor: James William Cooley was an American mathematician
intro: James Cooley (born 1926) is an American mathematician and computer scientist. He is known for his co-development of the Cooley-Tukey FFT algorithm, a fast algorithm for computing the discrete Fourier transform. Cooley's work in signal processing and algorithm design has had a significant impact on various fields, including telecommunications and image processing.
longBio: James William Cooley was an American mathematician. Cooley received a B.A. degree in 1949 from Manhattan College, Bronx, NY, an M.A. degree in 1951 from Columbia University, New York, NY, and a Ph.D. degree in 1961 in applied mathematics from Columbia University. He was a programmer on John von Neumann's computer at the Institute for Advanced Study, Princeton, NJ, from 1953 to 1956, where he notably programmed the Blackman–Tukey transformation.
achievement: With John W. Tukey, Known for co-developing the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) algorithm. His work revolutionized signal processing by enabling efficient computation of the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT), impacting a wide range of applications in fields like audio, image processing, and telecommunications.

intro: Edgar F. Codd (1923-2003) was a British computer scientist and mathematician. He is best known for his pioneering work in the field of relational databases and the invention of the relational model for database management systems. Codd's contributions have had a profound impact on the field of data management and database technology.
longBio: Edgar Frank "Ted" Codd was a British computer scientist who, while working for IBM, invented the relational model for database management, the theoretical basis for relational databases and relational database management systems. He won the 1981 ACM Turing Award.
achievement: Widely regarded as the father of the relational database. He formulated the fundamental principles of relational database management systems (RDBMS) and introduced the concept of data normalization. Codd's groundbreaking work on the relational model provided a theoretical framework for organizing and manipulating data, leading to the development of Structured Query Language (SQL) and the widespread adoption of relational databases. His contributions revolutionized the field of data management and laid the foundation for modern database systems. Codd received several prestigious awards for his work, including the Turing Award in 1981.

name: Per Martin-Löf
knownFor: Per Erik Rutger Martin-Löf is a Swedish logician, philosopher, and mathematical statistician
intro: Per Martin-Löf (born 1942) is a Swedish logician, mathematician, and philosopher. He is known for his foundational work in mathematical logic, particularly in the development of intuitionistic type theory. Martin-Löf's contributions to the field of logic and constructive mathematics have had a significant impact on computer science, programming language theory, and formal verification.
longBio: Per Erik Rutger Martin-Löf is a Swedish logician, philosopher, and mathematical statistician. He is internationally renowned for his work on the foundations of probability, statistics, mathematical logic, and computer science. Since the late 1970s, Martin-Löf's publications have been mainly in logic. In philosophical logic, Martin-Löf has wrestled with the philosophy of logical consequence and judgment, partly inspired by the work of Brentano, Frege, and Husserl. In mathematical logic, Martin-Löf has been active in developing intuitionistic type theory as a constructive foundation of mathematics; Martin-Löf's work on type theory has influenced computer science.
achievement: known for his foundational work in mathematical logic, type theory, and constructive mathematics. Martin-Löf's development of dependent type theory laid the groundwork for formal verification and programming languages. His contributions have had a profound impact on computer science, particularly in the areas of programming language design, proof theory, and formal methods.

Micali's research at the MIT Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory centers on cryptography and information security.
achievement: For transformative work that laid the complexity-theoretic foundations for the science of cryptography and in the process pioneered new methods for efficient verification of mathematical proofs in complexity theory.

intro: Srinivasa Ramanujan (1887-1920) was an Indian mathematician known for his extraordinary contributions to number theory and mathematical analysis. His work has had a lasting impact on various areas of mathematics and continues to inspire mathematicians to this day.
longBio: Srinivasa Ramanujan Iyengar was an Indian mathematician who worked during the early 20th century. He made substantial contributions to mathematical analysis, number theory, infinite series, and continued fractions, including solutions to mathematical problems then considered unsolvable.
achievement: Contribution to Number Theory, a fundamental aspect of Computer Science. An Indian mathematician who made remarkable contributions to number theory and mathematical analysis. His formulas, equations, and series have had a significant impact on mathematics. Ramanujan's work in partitions, theta functions, and collaborations with G.H. Hardy continue to inspire mathematicians worldwide.

name: Wesley A. Clark
knownFor: Wesley Allison Clark was an American physicist who is credited for designing the first modern
intro: Wesley A. Clark (born 1927) is an American computer scientist and engineer. He played a vital role in the development of computer systems and parallel computing. Clark co-designed the groundbreaking IBM Stretch supercomputer and contributed to the creation of the Burroughs B5000 computer system, which introduced innovative concepts like virtual memory and hardware support for multitasking.
longBio: Wesley Allison Clark was an American physicist who is credited for designing the first modern personal computer. He was also a computer designer and the main participant, along with Charles Molnar, in the creation of the LINC computer, which was the first minicomputer and shares with a number of other computers the claim to be the inspiration for the personal computer.
achievement: Designed LINC, the first functional computer scaled down and priced for individual users (1963). Many of its features are considered prototypes of essential elements of personal computers.

longBio: Niklaus Emil Wirth was a Swiss computer scientist. He designed several programming languages, including Pascal, and pioneered several classic topics in software engineering. name: Alan Kay
knownFor: Alan Curtis Kay is an American computer scientist who pioneered work on object-oriented programming and
intro: Alan Kay (born 1940) is an American computer scientist and pioneer in the field of personal computing. He is known for his work on object-oriented programming, graphical user interfaces, and the development of the Smalltalk programming language. Kay's contributions have greatly influenced modern computing and user interface design.
longBio:
Alan Curtis Kay is an American computer scientist who pioneered work on object-oriented programming and windowing graphical user interface (GUI) design. At Xerox PARC he led the design and development of the first modern windowed computer desktop interface. There he also led the development of the influential object-oriented programming language Smalltalk, both personally designing most of the early versions of the language and coining the term "object-oriented."
He has been elected a Fellow of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, the National Academy of Engineering, and the Royal Society of Arts. He received the Turing Award in 2003.
achievement: Known for his work on the development of object-oriented programming and graphical user interfaces. Kay is particularly associated with his work at Xerox PARC (Palo Alto Research Center), where he played a key role in the development of the Smalltalk programming language and the influential Dynabook concept, which envisioned a portable, interactive computer for educational purposes. Kay's ideas and research have greatly influenced the design of modern computing systems and user interfaces.

name: Masatoshi Shima
knownFor: Masatoshi Shima  is a Japanese electronics engineer
intro: Masatoshi Shima (born 1943) is a Japanese electrical engineer and computer architect. He was a key member of the team that developed the Intel 4004, the world's first commercially available microprocessor. Shima's contributions to microprocessor design have been instrumental in shaping the field of computer architecture and advancing the capabilities of modern computing devices.
longBio: Masatoshi Shima  is a Japanese electronics engineer. He was one of the architects of the world's first microprocessor, the Intel 4004. In 1968, Shima worked for Busicom in Japan, and did the logic design for a specialized CPU to be translated into three-chip custom chips. In 1969, he worked with Intel's Ted Hoff and Stanley Mazor to reduce the three-chip Busicom proposal into a one-chip architecture. In 1970, that architecture was transformed into a silicon chip, the Intel 4004, by Federico Faggin, with Shima's assistance in logic design.
achievement: Designed the Intel 4004, the first commercial microprocessor,as well as the Intel 8080, Zilog Z80 and Zilog Z8000 microprocessors, and the Intel 8259, 8255, 8253, 8257 and 8251 chips.

knownFor: Charles Patrick "Chuck" Thacker was an American pioneer computer designer
intro: Charles P. Thacker (1943-2017) was an American computer scientist and hardware designer. He played a key role in the development of personal computing and graphical user interfaces. Thacker co-invented the Xerox Alto, one of the first computers to feature a graphical user interface. His contributions have had a lasting impact on computer design and human-computer interaction.
longBio: Charles Patrick "Chuck" Thacker was an American pioneer computer designer. He designed the Xerox Alto, which is the first computer that used a mouse-driven graphical user interface (GUI). In 2009, he won the ACM Turing Award.
achievement: American computer scientist and electrical engineer who made significant contributions to the development of personal computing and networking technologies. He was a key member of the team at Xerox PARC (Palo Alto Research Center) that developed the Alto, an early personal computer, and the Ethernet, a widely used networking technology. Thacker also played a crucial role in the development of the Xerox Star, one of the first commercial workstations with a graphical user interface. He received several prestigious awards for his contributions, including the Turing Award in 2009, for his pioneering work in the design and realization of computer systems and networks.

name: Dana Scott
knownFor: Dana Stewart Scott is an American logician who is the Hillman University Professor emeritus of
intro: Dana Scott (born 1932) is an American mathematician and computer scientist. He is known for his contributions to the field of theoretical computer science, particularly in the area of semantics of programming languages. Scott's work on lambda calculus and denotational semantics has had a significant impact on the foundations of programming languages and mathematical logic.
longBio: Dana Stewart Scott is an American logician who is the Hillman University Professor emeritus of Computer Science, Philosophy, and Mathematical Logic at Carnegie Mellon University. He is now retired and lives in Berkeley, California. He and Michael O. Rabin won the 1976 ACM Turing Award for their work on automata theory, while his collaborative work with Christopher Strachey in the 1970s laid the foundations of modern approaches to the semantics of programming languages. He has also worked on modal logic, topology, and category theory.
achievement: The joint paper "Finite Automata and Their Decision Problems," which introduced the idea of nondeterministic machines, which has proved to be an enormously valuable concept. Their (Scott & Rabin) classic paper has been a continuous source of inspiration for subsequent work in this field.
funFacts: ["Rabin won the 1976 ACM Turing Award for their work on automata theory, while his collaborative work with Christopher Strachey in the 1970s laid the foundations of modern approaches to the semantics of programming languages."]

name: Adi Shamir
knownFor: Adi Shamir is an Israeli cryptographer and inventor
intro: Adi Shamir (born 1952) is an Israeli cryptographer and computer scientist. He is one of the co-creators of the RSA encryption algorithm, along with Ron Rivest and Leonard Adleman. Shamir has made significant contributions to the field of cryptography, particularly in the areas of secret sharing and the development of various cryptographic protocols.
longBio: Adi Shamir is an Israeli cryptographer and inventor. He is a co-inventor of the Rivest–Shamir–Adleman (RSA) algorithm, a co-inventor of the Feige–Fiat–Shamir identification scheme, one of the inventors of differential cryptanalysis and has made numerous contributions to the fields of cryptography and computer science. In 2002, Ron Rivest, Len Adleman, and he won the ACM Turing Award.
achievement: He is one of the pioneers of modern cryptography and is best known as a co-inventor of the RSA encryption algorithm, along with Ron Rivest and Leonard Adleman. Shamir has made significant contributions to the field of computer science, particularly in the areas of cryptography, computer security, and algorithms. His work has had a profound impact on the field and has been instrumental in ensuring the security of digital communication and transactions.

He co-developed the TCP/IP protocols, which revolutionized data transmission over computer networks and laid the foundation for the modern internet. Kahn has received numerous awards for his contributions to computer networking and is widely recognized as one of the key architects of the internet.
longBio: Robert Elliot Kahn is an American electrical engineer who, along with Vint Cerf, first proposed the Transmission Control Protocol (TCP) and the Internet Protocol (IP), the fundamental communication protocols at the heart of the Internet.

name: Alexander Stepanov
knownFor: Alexander Alexandrovich Stepanov is a Russian-American computer programmer, best known as an advocate of generic
intro: Alexander Stepanov (born 1950) is a Russian-American computer scientist and mathematician. He is known for his work in the development of the Standard Template Library (STL) and his contributions to generic programming. Stepanov's work on generic programming concepts and algorithms has had a significant impact on the field of software engineering and programming languages.
longBio: Alexander Alexandrovich Stepanov is a Russian-American computer programmer, best known as an advocate of generic programming and as the primary designer and implementer of the C++ Standard Template Library, which he started to develop around 1992 while employed at HP Labs. He had earlier been working for Bell Labs close to Andrew Koenig and tried to convince Bjarne Stroustrup to introduce something like Ada generics in C++. He is credited with the notion of concept.
achievement: Known for his work on generic programming and the development of the Standard Template Library (STL). He is widely recognized for his contributions to the C++ programming language and his efforts in promoting the use of generic algorithms and data structures. Stepanov's work has had a profound impact on software development practices and has helped improve the efficiency and reliability of programming. He has received several awards and honors for his contributions to the field of computer science.
funFacts: ["He is the author (with Paul McJones) of Elements of Programming, a book that grew out of a \"Foundations of Programming\" course that Stepanov taught at Adobe Systems (while employed there)."]

name: Michael Stonebraker
knownFor: Michael Ralph Stonebraker is an American computer scientist specializing in database systems
intro: Michael Stonebraker (born 1943) is an American computer scientist and database technology pioneer. He has made significant contributions to the field of database management systems, including the development of INGRES, Postgres, and VoltDB. Stonebraker's work has greatly influenced the design and functionality of modern database systems, earning him numerous awards and honors.
longBio: Michael Ralph Stonebraker is an American computer scientist specializing in database systems. Through a series of academic prototypes and commercial startups, Stonebraker's research and products are central to many relational databases. He is also the founder of many database companies, including Ingres Corporation, Illustra, Paradigm4, StreamBase Systems, Tamr, Vertica, VoltDB and Hopara, and served as chief technical officer of Informix. For his contributions to database research, Stonebraker received the 2014 Turing Award, often described as "the Nobel Prize for computing."
achievement: Revolutionized the field of database management systems (DBMSs) and founded multiple successful database companies

name: Stephen Furber
knownFor: Stephen Byram Furber is an English computer scientist, mathematician and hardware engineer, and Emeritus ICL
intro: Stephen Furber (born 1953) is a British computer scientist and one of the pioneers of the field of neuromorphic engineering. He led the team that designed and developed the SpiNNaker (Spiking Neural Network Architecture) system, a massively parallel computer architecture inspired by the human brain. Furber's work has advanced the understanding and implementation of brain-inspired computing.
longBio: Stephen Byram Furber is an English computer scientist, mathematician and hardware engineer, and Emeritus ICL Professor of Computer Engineering in the Department of Computer Science at the University of Manchester, UK. After completing his education at the University of Cambridge, he spent the 1980s at Acorn Computers, where he was a principal designer of the BBC Micro and the ARM 32-bit RISC microprocessor. As of 2023, over 250 billion ARM chips have been manufactured, powering much of the world's mobile computing and embedded systems, everything from sensors to smartphones to servers.
achievement: Known for his work in computer architecture. He co-designed the ARM microprocessor architecture, widely used in electronic devices. Furber led the SpiNNaker project for brain simulation and has made contributions to neural engineering, advancing the fields of computational neuroscience and neuromorphic systems.

name: Radia Perlman
knownFor: Radia Joy Perlman is an American computer programmer and network engineer
intro: Radia Perlman (born 1951) is an American computer scientist known as the "Mother of the Internet" for her work in the development of the Spanning Tree Protocol (STP) and her contributions to the field of network routing and bridging. Perlman's work has been instrumental in the scalability and reliability of modern computer networks.
longBio: Radia Joy Perlman is an American computer programmer and network engineer. She is a major figure in assembling the networks and technology to enable what we now know as the Internet. She is most famous for her invention of the Spanning Tree Protocol (STP), which is fundamental to the operation of network bridges, while working for Digital Equipment Corporation, thus earning her nickname "Mother of the Internet" a title that she did not like. Her innovations have made a huge impact on how networks self-organize and move data. She also made large contributions to many other areas of network design and standardization – for example, enabling today's link-state routing protocols to be more robust, scalable, and easy to manage.
achievement: Invented the Spanning Tree Protocol (STP), which is fundamental to the operation of network bridges, while working for Digital Equipment Corporation. Has done extensive and innovative research, particularly on encryption and networking. She received the USENIX Lifetime Achievement Award in 2007, among numerous others.

name: Barbara Liskov
knownFor: Barbara Jane Liskov is an American computer scientist who has made pioneering contributions to programming
intro: Barbara Liskov (born 1939) is an American computer scientist and recipient of the Turing Award. She is known for her groundbreaking work in programming languages, particularly the development of the CLU programming language and her contributions to the concept of abstract data types. Liskov's work has had a lasting impact on software engineering and programming language design.
longBio: Barbara Jane Liskov is an American computer scientist who has made pioneering contributions to programming languages and distributed computing. Her notable work includes the introduction of abstract data types and the accompanying principle of data abstraction, along with the Liskov substitution principle, which applies these ideas to object-oriented programming, subtyping, and inheritance. Her work was recognized with the 2008 Turing Award, the highest distinction in computer science.
achievement: Known for her groundbreaking contributions to the field of programming languages and software engineering. She is particularly renowned for her work on data abstraction and the development of the Liskov substitution principle, a fundamental concept in object-oriented programming. Liskov's research on programming language design and software engineering has greatly influenced the industry, leading to more reliable and maintainable software systems. She has received numerous awards for her contributions, including the Turing Award in 2008, becoming the first woman to receive this prestigious honor. Liskov has also made significant contributions to the advancement and representation of women in computer science.

intro: Shafi Goldwasser (born 1958) is an American-Israeli computer scientist and recipient of the Turing Award. She has made significant contributions to cryptography, computational complexity theory, and computational number theory. Goldwasser's work on interactive proofs and zero-knowledge proofs has revolutionized the field of cryptography and has had a profound impact on the security of modern computer systems.
longBio: Shafrira Goldwasser is an Israeli-American computer scientist. She is the RSA Professor of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science at the Massachusetts Institute of Technology, a professor of mathematical sciences at the Weizmann Institute of Science, the former director of the Simons Institute for the Theory of Computing, and co-founder and chief scientist of Duality Technologies. In 2012, she and Silvio Micali won the ACM Turing Award.

name: Satoshi Nakamoto
knownFor: Satoshi Nakamoto is the name used by the presumed pseudonymous person or persons who developed
intro: Satoshi Nakamoto is the pseudonymous person or group credited with inventing Bitcoin, the first decentralized cryptocurrency, in a whitepaper published in 2008. The true identity of Satoshi Nakamoto remains unknown. The concept and technology introduced by Nakamoto revolutionized the financial industry and sparked the growth of the cryptocurrency ecosystem.
longBio: Satoshi Nakamoto is the name used by the presumed pseudonymous person or persons who developed bitcoin, authored the bitcoin white paper, and created and deployed bitcoin's original reference implementation. As part of the implementation, Nakamoto also devised the first blockchain database. Nakamoto was active in the development of bitcoin until December 2010.
achievement: The anonymous creator or creators of Bitcoin, the first peer-to-peer digital currency. Nakamoto's 2008 white-paper introduced the concept of the blockchain, a database structure that allows full trust in the decentralized and distributed public transaction ledger of the cryptocurrency.

intro: Leslie Valiant (born 1949) is a British computer scientist and recipient of the Turing Award. Valiant's work has advanced our understanding of machine learning and the computational foundations of intelligence.
longBio: Leslie Gabriel Valiant is a British American computer scientist and computational theorist. He was born to a chemical engineer father and a translator mother. He is currently the T. Jefferson Coolidge Professor of Computer Science and Applied Mathematics at Harvard University. Valiant was awarded the Turing Award in 2010, having been described by the A.C.M. as a heroic figure in theoretical computer science and a role model for his courage and creativity in addressing some of the deepest unsolved problems in science; in particular for his "striking combination of depth and breadth".
achievement: Transformative contributions to the theory of computation, including the theory of probably approximately correct (PAC) learning, the complexity of enumeration and of algebraic computation, and the theory of parallel and distributed computing.

name: Susan L. Graham
knownFor: Susan Lois Graham is an American computer scientist
intro: Susan L. Graham is an American computer scientist known for her contributions to programming languages and software engineering. She has made significant contributions to the field of compiler optimization and software development tools. Graham's research has focused on program analysis, parallel computing, and software engineering methodologies, influencing the design and optimization of programming languages and compilers.
longBio: Susan Lois Graham is an American computer scientist. Graham is the Pehong Chen Distinguished Professor Emerita in the Computer Science Division of the Department of Electrical Engineering and Computer Sciences at the University of California, Berkeley.
achievement: Awarded the 2009 IEEE John von Neumann Medal for "contributions to programming language design and implementation and for exemplary service to the discipline of computer science”

name: Jack Cole
knownFor: Jack Cole may refer to:Jack Cole (artist) (1914–1958), American comic book artist and cartoonist Jack
intro:
Jack Cole's academic journey, which spanned over four decades, had a belated start due to the interruptions of World War II. He was caught in a 'friendly fire' incident during a training exercise, forcing him to relearn writing with his left hand before he could embark on his tertiary education in his early twenties. Despite these challenges, Jack excelled, earning a first-class honours degree in Mathematics from University College, London. This was followed by receiving the prestigious Mayer de Rothschild Scholarship for his MSc. Under the guidance of his mentor, Professor Harold Davenport, and the support of a London University Scholarship, Jack completed his PhD in the theory of numbers in 1952.

Born within the echoes of Bow Bells, Jack spent a significant portion of his academic career in Scotland, eventually identifying himself as a "Scot by marriage". He began as a lecturer at the then Heriot-Watt College in Edinburgh (1952-56), before moving on to Queen’s College, Dundee (1956-62).

However, the allure of emerging computer technology gradually nudged Jack towards a shift in his career. He moved back to England to become the Director of the Computing Laboratory at the University of Leicester (1962-65). But England's grip on him was short-lived, and he found himself back in Scotland in 1965, serving as the Director of the Computing Laboratory and Reader in Computational Science at the University of St Andrews.

achievement: Jack's success in research was greatly influenced by his background in mathematics and deep interest in computers. His work revolved around space filling curves, an area that had previously captivated the likes of mathematical giants such as Peano and Hilbert. Jack's innovative approach allowed these curves to be utilized for video data compression. His substantial contributions to the field were reflected in numerous publications, including the groundbreaking invention of Murray polygons. Although his research was considered groundbreaking at the time of publication, it has now been industrialized, with the BBC being one of the notable adopters. Jack's prolific career resulted in the publication of three books and over fifty academic papers.

name: Steve Wozniak
knownFor: Stephen Gary Wozniak, also known by his nickname Woz, is an American technology entrepreneur, electrical
intro: Stephen Gary Wozniak, commonly known as "Woz", is a renowned American technology entrepreneur, electronics engineer, and inventor who was instrumental in the personal computer revolution. Born on August 11, 1950, Wozniak co-founded Apple Inc. with Steve Jobs in 1976. The company would later become a global powerhouse, standing as the world's largest technology firm by revenue and market capitalization. Wozniak's technical genius can be witnessed in the creation of the Apple I and Apple II, the latter being one of the first successful mass-produced microcomputers. His influence extended to the development of the original Apple Macintosh. After leaving Apple in 1985, Wozniak continued to innovate, creating the first programmable universal remote and engaging in various technological and philanthropic endeavors, especially in K-12 education. Even after stepping down from active duties, he maintains a ceremonial association with Apple. Wozniak's influence has expanded over the years to various areas including GPS, telecommunications, technical education, and pop culture conventions.
longBio: Stephen Gary Wozniak, also known by his nickname Woz, is an American technology entrepreneur, electrical engineer, computer programmer, and inventor. In 1976, he co-founded Apple Computer with his early business partner Steve Jobs. Through his work at Apple in the 1970s and 1980s, he is widely recognized as one of the most prominent pioneers of the personal computer revolution.
achievement: He co-founded Apple Inc. with Steve Jobs, and single-handedly designed the Apple I and II computers, igniting the microcomputer revolution. Wozniak also created the first programmable universal remote and helped develop the initial graphical user interface. He contributed to founding the Electronic Frontier Foundation for digital rights and initiated the Silicon Valley Comic Con. His awards include the National Medal of Technology and induction into the Inventors Hall of Fame. A committed philanthropist, Wozniak has actively promoted education and technology accessibility.

name: Steve Jobs
knownFor: Steven Paul Jobs was an American businessman, inventor, and investor
intro: Steve Jobs (February 24, 1955 – October 5, 2011) was an American business magnate, inventor, and influential tech visionary. Co-founder, chairman, and CEO of Apple, he also served as the chairman and majority shareholder of Pixar, and the founder, chairman, and CEO of NeXT. Known for igniting the personal computer revolution alongside Steve Wozniak, Jobs' innovative streak reshaped multiple industries. Born in San Francisco and adopted shortly after birth, Jobs' journey led him from college dropout to co-founder of Apple, launching the groundbreaking Apple I and II computers and later the Macintosh. After a power struggle in 1985, he left Apple, only to return in 1997, leading the company's revival and the creation of culture-shifting products like the iMac, iPod, iPhone, and iPad. Jobs also contributed to the animation industry through Pixar, resulting in numerous celebrated films. Despite battling a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor, Jobs continued to innovate until his death in 2011, and was posthumously awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2022.
longBio: Steven Paul Jobs was an American businessman, inventor, and investor. A pioneer of the personal computer revolution of the 1970s and 1980s, Jobs co-founded Apple Inc. with his early business partner Steve Wozniak as Apple Computer Company in 1976. After the company's board of directors fired him in 1985, he founded NeXT the same year and purchased Pixar in 1986, becoming its chairman and majority shareholder until 2007. Jobs returned to Apple in 1997 as CEO, where he was closely involved with the creation and promotion of many of the company's most influential products until his resignation in 2011.
achievement: The iconic co-founder of Apple Inc., is widely acclaimed for revolutionizing the tech industry. His key accomplishments include launching the Apple I and II, pioneering graphical user interfaces with the Macintosh, and being ousted from Apple in 1985. His creation of NeXT led to fundamental internet technology, and his acquisition of Pixar changed animation forever. On returning to Apple in 1997, he resurrected it from near bankruptcy, introducing groundbreaking products like the iMac, iPod, iPhone, and iPad. His innovative spirit transformed how we use technology, profoundly shaping our digital-centric world before his death in 2011.
funFacts: ["Together, the duo gained fame and wealth a year later with the production and sale of the Apple II, one of the first highly successful mass-produced microcomputers.", "The Macintosh launched the desktop publishing industry in 1985 (for example, the Aldus PageMaker) with the addition of the Apple LaserWriter, the first laser printer to feature vector graphics and PostScript.", "Pixar produced the first computer-animated feature film, Toy Story (1995), and became a leading animation studio, producing dozens of commercially successful and critically acclaimed films.", "In 2003, Jobs was diagnosed with a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor."]
